question: What is the primary purpose of the spectral density function in time series analysis?
options: To decompose a time series into its frequency components
To forecast future values of a time series
To test for stationarity in a time series
To identify the order of an ARMA model
explanation: The spectral density function reveals how the variance of a stationary process is distributed across different frequency components, allowing us to understand periodic behaviors.

question: For a stationary time series process, how is the spectral density function f(ω) related to the autocovariance function γ(k)?
options: They are a Fourier transform pair
f(ω) is the derivative of γ(k)
γ(k) is the integral of f(ω)
They are not directly related
explanation: The spectral density function is the Fourier transform of the autocovariance function, and vice versa, the autocovariance is the inverse Fourier transform of the spectral density. This is a fundamental relationship in spectral analysis.

question: Which of the following statements about the spectral density of a white noise process is TRUE?
options: It is constant across all frequencies
It is zero for all frequencies
It peaks at the frequency ω=0
It has a single spike at a specific frequency
explanation: A white noise process has no autocorrelation (γ(k)=0 for k≠0), meaning its variance is equally distributed across all frequencies. This results in a flat, constant spectral density function.

question: An AR(1) process, X_t = φX_{t-1} + ε_t, is known to exhibit smoother oscillations when φ is negative. How would this be reflected in its spectral density?
options: The spectral density would have a peak at a frequency ω ≠ 0
The spectral density would be flat like white noise
The spectral density would peak at ω = 0
The spectral density would be zero for all ω > 0
explanation: A negative φ₁ parameter in an AR(1) model introduces negative autocorrelation at lag 1, which produces oscillatory behavior. This manifests as a peak in the spectral density at a frequency other than zero, indicating a dominant cyclic component.

question: Consider the spectral density functions of two processes: a low-frequency AR(2) process modeling a business cycle and a high-frequency process modeling hourly temperature changes. Which property will most clearly distinguish them?
options: The location of the peak(s) along the frequency axis
The total area under the spectral density curve
The value of the spectral density at ω=0
The number of peaks in the spectral density
explanation: The location of the peak indicates the dominant frequency of the oscillation. A business cycle (low-frequency) will have a peak near 0, while an hourly cycle (high-frequency) will have a peak at a much higher frequency. The total area under the curve represents the process variance and would be similar only if their variances were equal.
